title: Copy List with Random Pointer
code: for (Node cur = head; cur != null; cur = cur.next)
for (Node cur = head; cur != null; cur = cur.next)

frames.caption: A list where each node has a next AND a random pointer (random can point anywhere). Deep-copy it so no clone pointer references any original node.
frames.note: The naive copy fails because random can point to a node we have not created yet (here A.random -> C and B.random -> A). The clearest fix is a hash map original -> clone: build all clones first, then translate every pointer through the map. O(n) time, O(n) space.

frames.caption: head is not null, and we create an empty map. The map will let pass 2 turn any original pointer into the matching clone.
frames.note: Seeding a Dictionary<Node, Node> up front is the key data structure. Looking up an original node returns its clone in O(1), which is exactly what both the next-wiring and the random-wiring need.

frames.caption: Pass 1 — clone A (val 7): make a fresh node A' and record A -> A' in the map. next/random stay null for now.
frames.note: Pass 1 does ONE thing: copy the value into a brand-new node and record original -> clone in the map. We deliberately leave next and random null for now. Why two passes? Because random can point to a node that appears LATER in the list (or even back at itself); if we tried to wire random while cloning, the target clone might not exist yet. Cloning everyone first guarantees every clone exists before any pointer is wired.

frames.caption: Pass 1 — clone B (val 13): make a fresh node B' and record B -> B' in the map. next/random stay null for now.
frames.note: Pass 1 does ONE thing: copy the value into a brand-new node and record original -> clone in the map. We deliberately leave next and random null for now. Why two passes? Because random can point to a node that appears LATER in the list (or even back at itself); if we tried to wire random while cloning, the target clone might not exist yet. Cloning everyone first guarantees every clone exists before any pointer is wired.

frames.caption: Pass 1 — clone C (val 11): make a fresh node C' and record C -> C' in the map. next/random stay null for now.
frames.note: Pass 1 does ONE thing: copy the value into a brand-new node and record original -> clone in the map. We deliberately leave next and random null for now. Why two passes? Because random can point to a node that appears LATER in the list (or even back at itself); if we tried to wire random while cloning, the target clone might not exist yet. Cloning everyone first guarantees every clone exists before any pointer is wired.

frames.caption: Pass 1 done: every original now has a clone in the map (A->A', B->B', C->C'). The clones exist but are still unwired — no next, no random.
frames.note: This is the precondition pass 2 relies on. Because all clones already exist, wiring any pointer is just one map lookup — even a random pointer to a node further down the list resolves to a clone that is already built.

frames.caption: Pass 2 — wire A'.next: orig A.next = B, so look up map[B] = B' and set A'.next = B'.
frames.note: Pass 2 wires the new node. We never copy a pointer directly — an original pointer references an ORIGINAL node, but the clone must reference the corresponding CLONE. So for each link we look up the original target in the map and use the clone it maps to: clone.next = map[orig.next], clone.random = map[orig.random]. A null target maps to null. This translation through the map is the whole trick.

frames.caption: Wire A'.random: orig A.random = C, so map[C] = C' gives the clone-side target — set A'.random = C'.
frames.note: Here is the payoff: orig.random can point anywhere, but map[orig.random] is already a fully-created clone (pass 1 built them all), so we resolve the arbitrary jump in O(1) and point the clone-side random at the clone-side target. No special-casing of forward vs. backward vs. self references is needed.

frames.caption: Pass 2 — wire B'.next: orig B.next = C, so look up map[C] = C' and set B'.next = C'.
frames.note: Pass 2 wires the new node. We never copy a pointer directly — an original pointer references an ORIGINAL node, but the clone must reference the corresponding CLONE. So for each link we look up the original target in the map and use the clone it maps to: clone.next = map[orig.next], clone.random = map[orig.random]. A null target maps to null. This translation through the map is the whole trick.

frames.caption: Wire B'.random: orig B.random = A, so map[A] = A' gives the clone-side target — set B'.random = A'.
frames.note: Here is the payoff: orig.random can point anywhere, but map[orig.random] is already a fully-created clone (pass 1 built them all), so we resolve the arbitrary jump in O(1) and point the clone-side random at the clone-side target. No special-casing of forward vs. backward vs. self references is needed.

frames.caption: Pass 2 — wire C'.next: orig C.next is null, so C'.next = null (tail of the clone list).
frames.note: Pass 2 wires the new node. We never copy a pointer directly — an original pointer references an ORIGINAL node, but the clone must reference the corresponding CLONE. So for each link we look up the original target in the map and use the clone it maps to: clone.next = map[orig.next], clone.random = map[orig.random]. A null target maps to null. This translation through the map is the whole trick.

frames.caption: Wire C'.random: orig C.random is null, so C'.random = null.
frames.note: Pass 2 wires the new node. We never copy a pointer directly — an original pointer references an ORIGINAL node, but the clone must reference the corresponding CLONE. So for each link we look up the original target in the map and use the clone it maps to: clone.next = map[orig.next], clone.random = map[orig.random]. A null target maps to null. This translation through the map is the whole trick.

frames.caption: Both passes done. The clone list is fully wired and independent of the original — return map[head] = A'.
frames.note: The deep copy is complete: every clone next and random points only at other clones, never at an original. The map can now be discarded. This two-pass map approach is the one to reach for and explain first; an O(1)-space variant interleaves clones between originals, but it is trickier and rarely worth it under interview pressure.
